text: The surgeon inserts trocars along the umbilical line, with the left and right lateral trocars positioned higher than usual due to the large size of the uterus. Bipolar energy and an ultrasonic device are used to open the uterine serosa, coagulating small vessels to maintain a bloodless working area. The myometrial capsule is coagulated and cut to reveal the myometrial dissection plane.

The surgeon performs hydro dissection by inserting an irrigator device between the myoma and uterine serosa, injecting liquid to delineate the dissection plane. Using a 10mm forceps through the lateral trocar, the surgeon applies lateral and cranial traction to extract the myoma from its bed. The assistant uses bipolar energy to cut and coagulate connective tissue and surrounding blood vessels, achieving a bloodless procedure. The myoma is nearly completely extracted from its bed.

The surgeon uses an ultrasonic device to remove the remaining part of the myoma. The myoma is placed in the right hypochondrium. Hemostasis is achieved by placing stitches with a 14mm needle and Vicryl suture, starting laterally to the uterine scar, approximately 3-4 centimeters on the right. The same procedure is repeated on the opposite side, invaginating the healthy myometrium into the myoma bed to reduce the risk of intrauterine hematoma. The rudder knot technique is used for efficient hemostatic suturing.

The surgeon repeats the suturing maneuver on the upper part of the uterine scar, placing stitches four centimeters lateral to the edge. Care is taken to avoid including the fallopian tube in the stitch. The closure is performed using the extracorporeal knotting technique with a rudder knot, ensuring efficient hemostasis. The blanching of the scar edge indicates good ischemia. The final knot is placed on the caudal part of the scar, with tissue invagination into the myoma bed.

The surgeon invaginates tissue inside the myoma bed before locking the knot, placing an extra knot more lateral than previous stitches for improved invagination. Intracorporeal knotting is used for better control, visibly achieving the desired anatomical result. The suturing of the myoma is completed, and the procedure progresses to morselation.

The surgeon demonstrates the final aspect of the suture, ensuring all bleeding is removed from the abdominal cavity. The morcellation of the large myoma begins, initially without an endoscopic bag due to its size. After removing half of the myoma, an endoscopic bag is utilized. The morcellation process takes fourteen minutes.
